tracks.title: Beginner
tracks.description: Set up the AI tools every builder uses. From zero to your first working agent.

lessons.title: Setting up Claude Code
lessons.oneLineDescription: Install Anthropic's terminal coding assistant and run your first command.

steps.title: Check that you have Node 18+
steps.body:
Claude Code is distributed as an npm package and requires Node 18 or newer. Most modern Macs already have Node installed.

Run this in your terminal:

```bash
node --version
```

You should see something like `v20.10.0` or higher. If you see a version older than `v18`, install the latest Node from [nodejs.org](https://nodejs.org). If `node` is not found, you don't have Node installed yet — install it the same way.
steps.validationHint: node --version prints v18.0.0 or higher

steps.title: Get an Anthropic API key
steps.body:
Claude Code authenticates against your Anthropic account. If you don't have one yet, create it at [console.anthropic.com](https://console.anthropic.com).

Once signed in, go to **Settings → API Keys → Create Key**. Name the key something like `claude-code-laptop` so you can identify it later, then copy the key value (it starts with `sk-ant-`). You will see this key exactly once — paste it somewhere safe before closing the dialog.

Claude Code will read this key the first time you run it, so don't worry about exporting an environment variable yourself.
steps.validationHint: you have a key starting with sk-ant- saved in a password manager or note

steps.title: Install Claude Code globally
steps.body:
Install the CLI as a global npm package:

```bash
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
```

If this fails with an `EACCES` permission error, do NOT run with `sudo`. Instead, configure npm to install global packages into your home directory:

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.npm-global
npm config set prefix '~/.npm-global'
export PATH=~/.npm-global/bin:$PATH
```

Add that last line to your `~/.zshrc` or `~/.bashrc` so it persists across terminal sessions. Then re-run the install command.
steps.validationHint: command completes without errors

steps.title: Verify the install
steps.body:
Confirm Claude Code is on your PATH:

```bash
claude --version
```

You should see a version number like `1.5.0` or higher. If you see `command not found`, your shell isn't picking up the npm global bin directory — restart your terminal, then try again.
steps.validationHint: claude --version prints a version string

steps.title: Run claude in a project directory
steps.body:
Navigate to any code project on your machine — even a tiny one will do.

```bash
cd ~/Desktop
mkdir hello-claude && cd hello-claude
echo 'console.log("hi")' > index.js
claude
```

The first time you run `claude`, it will prompt you to paste your Anthropic API key (the one from step 2). Paste it and press Enter. The key is saved to `~/.claude/credentials.json` with file permissions `600` so only your user account can read it.
steps.validationHint: claude prompts for an API key the first time, then opens an interactive prompt

steps.title: Send your first message
steps.body:
With Claude Code running in the `hello-claude` directory, type a request:

```
add a new function to index.js that returns the current time in ISO format
```

Claude will explain what it is about to do, ask for permission to edit `index.js`, and then write the change. Say yes to the permission prompt.

Close Claude Code with `Ctrl+C`, then check the file:

```bash
cat index.js
```

You should see the original `console.log("hi")` plus the new function Claude added.
steps.validationHint: index.js now has both the original line and a function returning an ISO timestamp

steps.title: You're done
steps.body:
You now have Claude Code installed, authenticated, and running in your local projects. From here, the next lesson ("Setting up Cursor") covers the editor-based AI workflow as a complement to the terminal-based one you just installed.

To run Claude Code on any future project, just `cd` into the directory and run `claude`. It picks up the credentials from the first run automatically.

lessons.title: Setting up Cursor
lessons.oneLineDescription: Install Cursor and wire up Claude as your primary model.

steps.title: Download Cursor
steps.body:
Cursor is an AI-first code editor — visually a fork of VS Code, but with deep AI integration baked in. Grab the macOS build from [cursor.com](https://cursor.com).

Open the downloaded `Cursor.dmg`, drag `Cursor.app` to your `Applications` folder, then launch it. macOS will warn that the app was downloaded from the internet on first launch — click Open to bypass.
steps.validationHint: Cursor.app is in /Applications and launches without an error dialog

steps.title: Sign in or create an account
steps.body:
On first launch Cursor asks you to sign in. You can sign in with GitHub, Google, or email — pick whichever account you'll use long-term, since your editor settings sync to it.

The free tier includes a generous monthly quota of fast Claude / GPT requests, plus unlimited slow-model fallback. You don't need a paid plan to follow this lesson.
steps.validationHint: Cursor's command palette (⌘+K) shows your account in the top-right

steps.title: Pin Claude as the default model
steps.body:
Open Cursor's settings: **⌘,** → **Models**.

In the model picker, find `claude-sonnet-4-6` (or the latest Claude Sonnet variant) and toggle it on. Drag it to the top of the enabled list — Cursor uses the topmost enabled model as the default for ⌘+K and the chat panel.

If Claude isn't in the list, you may need to add an Anthropic API key in **Settings → Models → Anthropic** to unlock the full Claude family. Cursor's free tier includes Claude access through Cursor's own pool, but BYOK gives you priority and avoids hitting team rate limits.
steps.validationHint: the chat panel header shows 'claude-sonnet-4-6' (or similar) as the active model

steps.title: Open a project and try ⌘+K
steps.body:
From the welcome screen, click **Open Folder** and pick any code directory (the `hello-claude` folder from the previous lesson works perfectly).

Once the project is open, click into any file and press **⌘+K**. A small inline prompt appears. Type:

```
add a JSDoc comment explaining what this file does
```

Cursor proposes a diff. Press **⌘+Enter** to accept, **⌘+Backspace** to reject.
steps.validationHint: the file now has a JSDoc comment block at the top

steps.title: Try the chat panel
steps.body:
Press **⌘+L** to open Cursor's chat panel on the right. Unlike ⌘+K (inline single-file edit), the chat panel has access to your entire project and can make multi-file changes.

Ask it: `summarize this project's structure in 3 bullet points`. The first response should reference your actual files, not generic placeholders. If it doesn't, click the **+ Add Context** button at the top of the chat panel and select your project root.
steps.validationHint: Cursor's reply mentions specific files from your project by name

steps.title: You're done
steps.body:
You now have a fully-configured AI editor. ⌘+K is for surgical single-file edits; ⌘+L is for multi-file conversations. Claude is your default model.

From here, the Intermediate track teaches you to actually build things with these tools — starting with the Anthropic Memory API.

tracks.title: Intermediate
tracks.description: Once your tools are wired up — build something real. Agents, MCP, and prompt patterns.

lessons.title: Building your first agent with the Anthropic Memory API
lessons.oneLineDescription: Wire up persistent memory so your agent remembers prior conversations.

steps.title: Why memory matters for agents
steps.body:
A normal Claude API call is stateless — every request is independent and the model has no recollection of prior conversations. For chatbots that's fine, but for **agents** (programs that run over hours or days, taking actions on your behalf), you need persistence.

The Anthropic Memory API gives Claude a long-term memory store: facts, preferences, and conversation context that survive across separate API calls. This lesson wires up the simplest possible agent that uses it: a daily standup helper that remembers what you said yesterday.

steps.title: Set up a project directory
steps.body:
From your terminal:

```bash
mkdir standup-agent && cd standup-agent
npm init -y
npm install @anthropic-ai/sdk dotenv
```

Create a `.env` file with your Anthropic API key (the same `sk-ant-` key from the Claude Code lesson):

```
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-your-key-here
```

Add `.env` to `.gitignore` immediately so you never commit it.
steps.validationHint: package.json exists with anthropic + dotenv as dependencies

steps.title: Create a memory bucket
steps.validationHint: the script prints a bucket ID like `bkt_01xyz...`

steps.title: Write the agent loop
steps.validationHint: agent.js exists and references your bucket ID

steps.title: Seed it with a few entries
steps.body:
Run the agent three times to give it some history:

```bash
node agent.js "Today I shipped the curriculum schema. Tomorrow I want to wire up tutor."
node agent.js "Today I ran into a UserDefaults bug — fixed it. Tomorrow I'll start the Worker."
node agent.js "Today the Worker landed. Tomorrow I'll write iOS tutor UI."
```

Each call writes the user's message + Claude's reply into your memory bucket. After three days of entries, the bucket has enough state to summarize.
steps.validationHint: all three calls return text replies without error

steps.title: Ask it to recall
steps.body:
Now query the agent without any new content:

```bash
node agent.js "What have I been working on this week?"
```

Claude should produce a summary like:

> This week you shipped the curriculum schema, fixed a UserDefaults bug, and landed the tutor Worker. You're currently working on the iOS tutor UI.

If the reply is generic ("I don't have memory of prior conversations"), the bucket isn't connected — re-check the `BUCKET_ID` constant and the `memory:` block in `agent.js`.
steps.validationHint: Claude's reply references specific things from the prior 3 messages

steps.title: What you just did
steps.body:
You built a stateful agent. The Memory API handles all the heavy lifting — vector indexing, retrieval, context-window management — so you ship a 30-line script that behaves like an app with a database.

From here: try wiring this same pattern into a Discord bot, a CLI assistant, or a daily journaling app. The bucket is the persistence layer; everything else is just UI.

lessons.title: Wiring up MCP for Claude Desktop
lessons.oneLineDescription: Connect Claude Desktop to your local tools via the Model Context Protocol.

steps.title: What MCP is (and why it matters)
steps.body:
Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that lets Claude interact with external tools — file systems, databases, APIs, local processes — without you having to paste context into the chat window manually.

Claude Desktop has built-in MCP support. Once you wire it up, Claude can read files from your project, query a local SQLite database, or call a custom tool you've built, all from the chat interface.

steps.title: Install Claude Desktop
steps.body:
Download and install Claude Desktop from [claude.ai/download](https://claude.ai/download). Sign in with your Anthropic account.

Verify it works:

```bash
open -a 'Claude'
```

You should see the Claude Desktop chat window.
steps.validationHint: Claude Desktop opens and shows a chat input

steps.title: Open the MCP config file
steps.body:
Claude Desktop's MCP configuration lives in a JSON file. Open it:

```bash
open ~/Library/Application\ Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json
```

If the file doesn't exist yet:

```bash
mkdir -p ~/Library/Application\ Support/Claude
touch ~/Library/Application\ Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json
echo '{}' > ~/Library/Application\ Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json
```
steps.validationHint: claude_desktop_config.json opens in your editor

steps.title: Add the filesystem MCP server
steps.validationHint: claude_desktop_config.json saved with the mcpServers block

steps.title: Restart Claude Desktop and verify
steps.body:
Quit and reopen Claude Desktop — MCP servers only load on startup.

In the Claude Desktop chat, look for a tools icon (🔧) or a plug icon near the message input. Click it to see the connected MCP tools. You should see "filesystem" listed with read/write tools available.

Test it by asking Claude:

```
List the files on my Desktop
```

Claude should respond with your actual Desktop file names — not a hallucinated list.
steps.validationHint: Claude lists real filenames from your Desktop folder

steps.title: You're done
steps.body:
You've wired Claude Desktop to your local filesystem via MCP. From here you can add more MCP servers — databases, APIs, Git, web search. The full list of community MCP servers is at [github.com/modelcontextprotocol/servers](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/servers).

The Advanced track shows how to build your own MCP server and call it from agents.

tracks.title: Advanced
tracks.description: Production-grade AI engineering. Parallel agents, eval harnesses, and serverless deployment.

lessons.title: Setting up async parallel agents (Cursor 3.2 style)
lessons.oneLineDescription: Run multiple Cursor agent sessions simultaneously to parallelize independent code tasks.

steps.title: What parallel agents actually means
steps.body:
A "parallel agent" in the Cursor context means running two or more independent Cursor chat sessions, each with a different slice of your codebase, simultaneously. You're not spawning sub-agents in code — you're using Cursor's multi-window support to work two independent tasks at once.

This is useful when you have two tasks that don't share files — e.g., implementing a new API endpoint AND writing tests for an existing module. Both can run in parallel without merge conflicts.

steps.title: Create a second Cursor window
steps.body:
In Cursor: **File → New Window** (or `⌘+Shift+N`). This opens a second, fully independent Cursor instance with its own chat history.

Open each window to a **different directory** within your project — or the same root, but commit to working in non-overlapping files. The goal is zero file-level conflict between the two sessions.
steps.validationHint: Two Cursor windows are open, each in a separate directory or file scope

steps.title: Decompose your task into parallel lanes
steps.body:
Good decomposition is the skill. Before launching both agents, write out your task split:

```
Window A — API layer:
  - Add POST /lessons endpoint
  - Add validation middleware
  - Write integration test for the endpoint

Window B — Data model:
  - Add LessonProgress table migration
  - Add UserLessonRepository class
  - Write unit tests for UserLessonRepository
```

The split works when: (a) no file appears in both lists, (b) neither lane blocks the other.

If Window A's output is required input for Window B (e.g., A defines an interface B implements), make A finish first, then hand off to B.

steps.title: Launch both agents simultaneously
steps.body:
In **Window A**: open the chat panel (`⌘+L`), write a precise task description for Lane A, and press Enter. Don't wait for it to finish.

In **Window B**: immediately open its chat panel and start Lane B.

Both agents are now running in parallel — Cursor allocates API requests independently per window. While A is writing the endpoint, B is writing the repository class.

Monitor both windows. If one finishes early, queue the next task in that window.
steps.validationHint: Both windows show active agent responses simultaneously

steps.title: Review and merge
steps.body:
Once both finish, review each change in its own window first, then use `git diff` to see the combined change:

```bash
git diff
```

Look for:
- **File conflicts**: same file touched by both (shouldn't happen if your decomposition was correct)
- **Import mismatches**: A imports something from B's new file before B committed it
- **Test failures**: run tests across both lanes

```bash
npm test
```

Fix any issues, then commit everything together:
steps.validationHint: git diff shows only the expected files from both lanes, tests pass

steps.title: You're done
steps.body:
You've run your first parallel agent session. The speed gain depends on the task — highly parallelizable work (independent API + independent tests) can cut wall-clock time roughly in half. Non-parallelizable work (anything where A blocks B) is unchanged.

The next Advanced lesson shows how to apply this same parallel pattern to Cloudflare Worker deployment, where the Worker code and the iOS client integration can be developed simultaneously.

lessons.title: Deploying a Cloudflare Worker for AI APIs
lessons.oneLineDescription: Set up wrangler, create a KV namespace, add secrets, and deploy your first Worker.

steps.title: What Cloudflare Workers give you
steps.body:
A Cloudflare Worker is a serverless runtime that runs your code on Cloudflare's global edge network — no server to manage, free tier covers millions of requests per month, sub-10ms cold starts, and a built-in KV store for lightweight persistence.

For AI apps, Workers are the right place to:
- Proxy API calls so your Anthropic key never ships to the client
- Rate-limit per user without a database
- Cache expensive upstream responses

This lesson deploys a minimal Worker that proxies Anthropic's API.

steps.title: Install wrangler
steps.body:
Wrangler is Cloudflare's CLI for Workers development. Install it globally:

```bash
npm install -g wrangler
```

Verify:

```bash
wrangler --version
```

Should print `3.x.x` or higher.
steps.validationHint: wrangler --version prints a version string

steps.title: Authenticate with Cloudflare
steps.body:
Log in to your Cloudflare account from the CLI. If you don't have one, create a free account at [dash.cloudflare.com](https://dash.cloudflare.com) first.

```bash
wrangler login
```

This opens your browser to an OAuth flow. Approve access. Wrangler writes the token to `~/.wrangler/config/default.toml`.
steps.validationHint: wrangler login completes without an error

steps.title: Scaffold a new Worker
steps.body:
Create a new Worker project:

```bash
mkdir my-ai-proxy && cd my-ai-proxy
npm create cloudflare@latest . -- --type hello-world --no-git --no-deploy
```

This generates `wrangler.toml`, `src/index.ts`, and `package.json`. Open `src/index.ts` — it's a hello-world Worker. You'll replace its body with the proxy logic.

Also add the Anthropic SDK:

```bash
npm install @anthropic-ai/sdk
```
steps.validationHint: wrangler.toml and src/index.ts exist in the project

steps.title: Add your Anthropic API key as a secret
steps.body:
Never put API keys in `wrangler.toml` or source code — they'd end up in your git history and on Cloudflare's dashboard for anyone with repo access to see.

Instead, use Wrangler secrets:

```bash
wrangler secret put ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
```

Paste your `sk-ant-...` key when prompted. The key is encrypted at rest in Cloudflare's infrastructure and injected into your Worker's environment at request time — never exposed in deploy output.
steps.validationHint: wrangler secret put completes with 'Success: Created the secret'

steps.title: Deploy
steps.body:
```bash
wrangler deploy
```

Wrangler bundles your TypeScript, uploads it, and prints the live URL:

```
Published my-ai-proxy (0.20 sec)
https://my-ai-proxy.<your-subdomain>.workers.dev
```

Test it with curl:

```bash
curl https://my-ai-proxy.<your-subdomain>.workers.dev
```

Should return `Hello World!` from the default template. From here, replace the handler body with your actual proxy logic and re-run `wrangler deploy` to push updates.
steps.validationHint: curl returns a 200 response from the workers.dev URL
